exercises.title: Classify performance tests
exercises.instructions:
Match each scenario to the correct test type (Load, Stress, Soak, Spike):

A: Simulate 500 Zerodha users for 8 hours — watching if response time slowly climbs
B: Ramp from 100 → 5000 Razorpay payment users until the server returns 503
C: Send 10,000 requests/second to Flipkart checkout for 10 seconds (Black Friday flash sale)
D: Simulate 1000 normal Zomato orders for 30 minutes — asserting p99 < 2s
exercises.expected_solution:
A: SOAK TEST
   Long duration (8 hours), normal load (500 users)
   Goal: detect memory leaks, connection pool exhaustion over time
   Signal of failure: p99 slowly increases from 200ms at hour 1 to 800ms at hour 8
   Zerodha: run overnight before Diwali to detect JVM heap growth

B: STRESS TEST
   Increasing load until failure (100 → 5000)
   Goal: find the breaking point and verify graceful degradation
   Signal: at which user count do errors start? Does it recover?
   Razorpay: finds DB connection pool exhausts at 2000 concurrent users

C: SPIKE TEST
   Sudden massive increase (100 → 10,000 in seconds)
   Goal: verify auto-scaling kicks in, circuit breakers hold
   Signal: does checkout survive? How long to recover?
   Flipkart: simulates 'sale goes live' traffic pattern

D: LOAD TEST
   Normal expected load, bounded duration, SLA assertion
   Goal: 'Does our system meet the SLA under normal traffic?'
   Signal: p99 < 2s with 1000 users for 30 minutes
   Zomato: run in CI, fail build if SLA breached

test_cases.expected:
A: SOAK TEST
   Long duration (8 hours), normal load (500 users)
   Goal: detect memory leaks, connection pool exhaustion over time
   Signal of failure: p99 slowly increases from 200ms at hour 1 to 800ms at hour 8
   Zerodha: run overnight before Diwali to detect JVM heap growth

B: STRESS TEST
   Increasing load until failure (100 → 5000)
   Goal: find the breaking point and verify graceful degradation
   Signal: at which user count do errors start? Does it recover?
   Razorpay: finds DB connection pool exhausts at 2000 concurrent users

C: SPIKE TEST
   Sudden massive increase (100 → 10,000 in seconds)
   Goal: verify auto-scaling kicks in, circuit breakers hold
   Signal: does checkout survive? How long to recover?
   Flipkart: simulates 'sale goes live' traffic pattern

D: LOAD TEST
   Normal expected load, bounded duration, SLA assertion
   Goal: 'Does our system meet the SLA under normal traffic?'
   Signal: p99 < 2s with 1000 users for 30 minutes
   Zomato: run in CI, fail build if SLA breached

hints: Soak: normal load, long time
Stress: find breaking point
Spike: sudden burst
Load: verify SLA at expected traffic

exercises.title: Complete the Gatling injection profile
exercises.instructions: Fill in the Gatling load injection profile.
hints: atOnceUsers: immediate spike (for spike test)
rampUsers: gradual increase (for load/stress test)
percentile3 = p99 in Gatling DSL (counterintuitive naming!)

exercises.title: Basic Gatling scenario for payment API
exercises.instructions:
Write a Gatling simulation class:
1. HTTP protocol: baseUrl http://localhost:8080, JSON accept header
2. Scenario: POST /api/payments with body {amount:5000, gateway:HDFC}
3. Check: status 200
4. Inject: atOnceUsers(10)
5. Assert: p99 < 1000ms
hints: extends Simulation: Gatling entry point
{ setUp(...) }: instance initializer block runs the simulation setup
pause(100ms): simulate user think time between requests

exercises.title: k6 load test script
exercises.instructions:
Write a k6 JavaScript load test for Zerodha trade API:
1. 100 virtual users for 30 seconds
2. GET /api/portfolio/{userId}
3. Assert: status 200, response time < 500ms
4. Threshold: p99 < 500ms, error rate < 1%
5. Explain: when to choose k6 vs Gatling
hints: thresholds: k6 fails the run if breached (exit code 1 → CI fails)
check(): assertion — logged but doesn't fail the run (thresholds do)
sleep(0.1): think time — simulates real user behavior between requests

exercises.title: Gatling ramp-up stress test
exercises.instructions:
Write a Gatling stress test for Razorpay payment API:
1. Ramp: 0 → 100 → 500 → 1000 → 2000 users (staircase)
2. Each step: 60 seconds
3. Assert: at 500 users, p99 < 500ms
4. Verify: system fails gracefully at 2000 users (503, not crash)
5. @SpringBootTest: simulation class compiles
hints: status().in(200, 503): stress test expects some 503s — don't mark them as failures
rampUsersPerSec: smooth ramp (not jump)
requestsPerSec().gte(100): verify system still processing (not completely hung)

exercises.title: Interpret Gatling HTML report metrics
exercises.instructions:
Analyze this Gatling report and determine if the SLA is met:

```
Request: POST /api/payments
Requests: 12450 total, 12386 OK (99.5%), 64 KO (0.5%)
Response time distribution:
  t < 200ms: 45%
  200 ≤ t < 500ms: 43%
  t ≥ 500ms: 12%

Percentiles:
  p50: 195ms
  p75: 312ms
  p95: 487ms
  p99: 623ms
  max: 2340ms

SLA: p99 < 500ms, error rate < 1%
```

1. Does it pass the p99 SLA?
2. Does it pass the error rate SLA?
3. What does the max=2340ms tell you?
4. What immediate fix would you try?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. p99 SLA (< 500ms):
   FAIL — p99 = 623ms > 500ms threshold
   12% of requests (12450 × 0.12 = 1494 requests) took ≥ 500ms
   The assertion global().responseTime().percentile3().lt(500) would FAIL
   The build would be blocked in CI

2. Error rate SLA (< 1%):
   PASS — 64 KO / 12450 total = 0.5% < 1%
   global().successfulRequests().percent().gt(99.0) PASSES (99.5% > 99.0%)

3. max = 2340ms:
   Outlier: one (or a few) requests took 2.34 seconds
   Possible causes:
     - Database query with missing index for an edge case
     - JVM garbage collection pause (G1GC stop-the-world)
     - Cold start for a specific code path (e.g., first request to a new DB shard)
     - Network blip to the payment gateway
   Action: check if max coincides with GC pause timestamps in the JVM logs

4. Immediate fixes to try:
   Priority 1: Find what's causing p99=623ms (23% over limit)
     - Profile: is it database? External API? Serialization?
     - APM tool (Datadog, Jaeger): look at traces for p99 requests
   Quick wins:
     - Add DB indexes for most common queries
     - Tune JVM: -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=100 (reduce GC pause)
     - Enable HTTP keep-alive (avoid TCP handshake per request)
     - Increase HikariCP connection pool (if DB wait is the bottleneck)
   After fix: re-run load test to confirm p99 drops below 500ms

test_cases.expected:
1. p99 SLA (< 500ms):
   FAIL — p99 = 623ms > 500ms threshold
   12% of requests (12450 × 0.12 = 1494 requests) took ≥ 500ms
   The assertion global().responseTime().percentile3().lt(500) would FAIL
   The build would be blocked in CI

2. Error rate SLA (< 1%):

   global().successfulRequests().percent().gt(99.0) PASSES (99.5% > 99.0%)

3. max = 2340ms:
   Outlier: one (or a few) requests took 2.34 seconds
   Possible causes:
     - Database query with missing index for an edge case
     - JVM garbage collection pause (G1GC stop-the-world)
     - Cold start for a specific code path (e.g., first request to a new DB shard)
     - Network blip to the payment gateway
   Action: check if max coincides with GC pause timestamps in the JVM logs

4. Immediate fixes to try:
   Priority 1: Find what's causing p99=623ms (23% over limit)
     - Profile: is it database? External API? Serialization?
     - APM tool (Datadog, Jaeger): look at traces for p99 requests
   Quick wins:
     - Add DB indexes for most common queries
     - Tune JVM: -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=100 (reduce GC pause)
     - Enable HTTP keep-alive (avoid TCP handshake per request)
     - Increase HikariCP connection pool (if DB wait is the bottleneck)
   After fix: re-run load test to confirm p99 drops below 500ms

hints: p99=623ms > 500ms threshold: SLA FAIL
0.5% error rate < 1% threshold: SLA PASS
max outlier: suspect GC pause or cold code path

exercises.title: Gatling feeder for realistic payment data
exercises.instructions:
Write a Gatling scenario using CSV feeder for realistic payment data:
1. payments.csv: merchantId,amount,gateway
2. Feeder feeds different data to each virtual user
3. Parameterize the POST body with feeder values
4. @SpringBootTest: feeder scenario compiles

expected_stdout_contains: Feeder: realistic data per virtual user

hints: csv().circular(): when feeder rows exhausted, start from beginning
#{merchantId}: Gatling session variable (injected by feeder)
Realistic data: prevents artificial server-side caching from skewing results

exercises.title: Performance regression test in CI
exercises.instructions:
Design a Gatling CI performance gate:
1. Baseline stored: p99=320ms, error_rate=0.2% (last release)
2. Current test: if p99 > 350ms (10% regression), fail the build
3. GitHub Actions workflow: run Gatling on every PR to main
4. @SpringBootTest: PerformanceRegressionChecker logic
hints: 10% regression: allow 320ms to become up to 352ms before failing
Gatling exit code 1: assertions fail → CI step fails → PR blocked
Store baseline: JSON file in repo, updated when baseline deliberately improved

exercises.title: k6 threshold configuration for different environments
exercises.instructions:
Write k6 options for 3 environments:
1. Development (local): lenient (p99 < 2000ms, 80% success)
2. Staging: moderate (p99 < 800ms, 95% success)
3. Production gate: strict (p99 < 300ms, 99.9% success)
4. Environment selected via env variable K6_ENV
5. @SpringBootTest: threshold logic
hints: __ENV.K6_ENV: k6 environment variable from shell
Production run: minimal VUs (1-5) — only validate, never load test production
p(50)<100: verify median is fast, not just p99

exercises.title: Diagnose performance bottleneck
exercises.instructions:
Zerodha portfolio API: Gatling load test (1000 users, 5 minutes). Results:

```
p50:  45ms  (excellent)
p75:  67ms  (excellent)
p95: 480ms  (borderline)
p99: 3200ms (way over SLA)
max: 8900ms
Error rate: 0.1%

JVM metrics during test:
  Heap: 512MB → 1.8GB (growing)
  GC pauses: every 90s, duration 2.5s
  Thread pool: 200/200 (maxed out)
  DB connections: 48/50 (near limit)
```

What are the 3 most likely bottlenecks and how would you fix each?
exercises.expected_solution:
BOTTLENECK 1: JVM GC Pauses (most likely cause of p99 = 3200ms)
   Evidence: GC every 90s, duration 2.5s → every ~90 seconds, ALL requests pause for 2.5s
   That 2.5s pause = 2500ms → directly explains p99 = 3200ms (2500ms GC + 700ms normal)
   Fix:
     - Increase heap: -Xmx4g (delay GC from every 90s to every 5 minutes)
     - Use G1GC with max pause: -XX:+UseG1GC -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=100
     - Heap growth 512MB → 1.8GB signals a memory leak → find and fix the leak
   Priority: HIGHEST — GC pauses directly cause the p99 spike

BOTTLENECK 2: Heap Memory Leak (512MB → 1.8GB growth)
   Evidence: heap grows from 512MB to 1.8GB over 5 minutes → leak
   If uncapped: after 20 minutes heap would hit -Xmx → OutOfMemoryError → crash
   Fix:
     - Memory profiler (VisualVM, async-profiler): find what's accumulating
     - Common causes: Caffeine cache without eviction, static collections growing,
       unclosed resources (DB connections, streams)
     - Check: @Cacheable without TTL or size limit (unbounded cache)

BOTTLENECK 3: Thread Pool at capacity (200/200)
   Evidence: 200/200 means ALL threads are in use
   When a new request arrives: waits in queue → increases response time
   Combined with GC pause: threads stuck waiting → queue overflows → 503
   Fix:
     - Increase: server.tomcat.threads.max=500 (if hardware supports it)
     - Or: switch to reactive (WebFlux) for non-blocking I/O (same threads handle more)
     - Root cause: why are threads taking long? → GC pause holding threads (back to fix 1)

DB connections (48/50) is a WARNING but not critical yet:
   At 50 connections: next requests wait for connection → adds latency
   Fix: increase HikariCP: spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=100

test_cases.expected:
BOTTLENECK 1: JVM GC Pauses (most likely cause of p99 = 3200ms)
   Evidence: GC every 90s, duration 2.5s → every ~90 seconds, ALL requests pause for 2.5s
   That 2.5s pause = 2500ms → directly explains p99 = 3200ms (2500ms GC + 700ms normal)
   Fix:
     - Increase heap: -Xmx4g (delay GC from every 90s to every 5 minutes)
     - Use G1GC with max pause: -XX:+UseG1GC -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=100
     - Heap growth 512MB → 1.8GB signals a memory leak → find and fix the leak
   Priority: HIGHEST — GC pauses directly cause the p99 spike

BOTTLENECK 2: Heap Memory Leak (512MB → 1.8GB growth)
   Evidence: heap grows from 512MB to 1.8GB over 5 minutes → leak
   If uncapped: after 20 minutes heap would hit -Xmx → OutOfMemoryError → crash
   Fix:
     - Memory profiler (VisualVM, async-profiler): find what's accumulating
     - Common causes: Caffeine cache without eviction, static collections growing,
       unclosed resources (DB connections, streams)
     - Check: @Cacheable without TTL or size limit (unbounded cache)

BOTTLENEACK 3: Thread Pool at capacity (200/200)
   Evidence: 200/200 means ALL threads are in use
   When a new request arrives: waits in queue → increases response time
   Combined with GC pause: threads stuck waiting → queue overflows → 503
   Fix:
     - Increase: server.tomcat.threads.max=500 (if hardware supports it)
     - Or: switch to reactive (WebFlux) for non-blocking I/O (same threads handle more)
     - Root cause: why are threads taking long? → GC pause holding threads (back to fix 1)

DB connections (48/50) is a WARNING but not critical yet:
   At 50 connections: next requests wait for connection → adds latency
   Fix: increase HikariCP: spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=100

hints: GC 2.5s pause → p99 3200ms: directly correlates (2500ms + 700ms normal)
Heap growth 512→1800MB: memory leak → fix before it crashes
Thread pool maxed: queuing adds latency on top of GC pauses

exercises.title: Complete Gatling load test with parameterized scenarios
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Gatling test for Razorpay payment flow:
1. Scenario 1 (70%): standard payment POST /api/payments
2. Scenario 2 (30%): refund GET /api/refunds/{paymentId}
3. Inject: rampUsers(100) over 60s
4. Assert: p99 < 500ms, success > 99%
5. @SpringBootTest: simulation compiles and docs test passes
hints: Multiple scenarios in setUp: parallel execution (both run simultaneously)
jsonPath('$.paymentId').saveAs(): capture response value for later steps
Realistic distribution: most apps have read-heavy vs write-heavy split

exercises.title: Soak test for memory leak detection
exercises.instructions:
Write a Gatling soak test and MemoryLeakDetector service:
1. Soak test: 200 users, 8 hours (simulate with 5-minute test here)
2. MemoryLeakDetector: takes heap snapshots every minute, alerts if growing > 50MB/min
3. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): growing heap detected, stable heap OK
hints: 3 snapshots growing: sustained trend (not just GC noise)
injectGrowthForTest: test helper for simulating heap states
250MB growth over 3 intervals >> 100MB threshold = leak detected

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay performance test suite
exercises.instructions:
Build complete performance test suite:
1. PaymentLoadSimulation: Gatling load test (100 users, 30s)
2. PaymentStressSimulation: ramp 10→500 users
3. PerformanceRegressionChecker: compare baseline vs current
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): regression check (pass, fail, soak leak)
5. All 3 pass
hints: 10% regression: 320 * 1.10 = 352ms max
400ms > 352ms: regression detected
heapGrowth 370-200=170MB > 150MB threshold: leak detected

exercises.title: Performance test data generation
exercises.instructions:
Build realistic test data generator for Zerodha load tests:
1. TradeDataGenerator: generates realistic NSE stock trades
2. Weighted distribution: RELIANCE 25%, TCS 20%, INFY 15%, others 40%
3. Amount: log-normal distribution (most trades ₹1K-50K, some ₹1L+)
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): distribution roughly correct, amounts in valid range
hints: Weighted pool: RELIANCE has 5 entries out of 20 = 25% probability
Log-normal: realistic distribution (most trades small, a few large)
Seeded Random(42): reproducible test data for debugging

exercises.title: Complete M25 T4 Performance Testing synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete M25 T4 synthesis:
1. PerformanceBaseline record (p99, errRate, heapMb)
2. PerformanceResult record (p99, errRate, heapMb)
3. PerformanceSuite: isAcceptable() + reportSummary()
4. RegressionHistory: store last 5 baselines, detect trend
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): pass, fail, trend up, regression alert
6. All 4 pass
exercises.starter_code: // Complete M25 T4 Performance Testing synthesis
hints: isRegressionTrend: strictly increasing (not just occasionally higher)
400 > 300*1.10=330: 33% regression → FAIL
290→310: last is higher but second wasn't → not a consistent trend
